fs-toc-hideurlhash="true" fs-toc-element="contents" fs-toc-offsettop="7.5rem" class="rich-text w-richtext"><p>Building a pricing page for tiered plans is straightforward. However, building one that handles usage limits, credits, add-ons, and real-time cost calculations is a different problem. We looked at 25 SaaS pricing page examples to see how other teams solved it.</p><div id="how-top-saas-companies-structure-their-pricing-pages" style="scroll-margin-top: 7.5rem;"><h2>How top SaaS companies structure their pricing pages</h2><p>Great SaaS pricing pages use <strong>clean comparison layouts</strong> with <strong>taglines or personas</strong> that help buyers spot their plan immediately. For usage-based pricing, interactive calculators show real costs upfront, so there’s no guessing and no surprises. <strong>FAQs are grouped by topic, and trust signals work in sequence:</strong> customer logos build credibility early, ROI stats close the deal later. </p><p>The actual infrastructure you need includes <strong>metering systems</strong> that track usage in real time, <a href="https://www.stigg.io/blog-posts/entitlements-untangled-the-modern-way-to-software-monetization" data-wf-native-id-path="119ecf52-243e-fdac-0df8-fb91309ff53e" data-wf-ao-click-engagement-tracking="true" data-wf-element-id="119ecf52-243e-fdac-0df8-fb91309ff53e"><strong>entitlement</strong></a><strong> layers</strong> that instantly grant or revoke features, and <strong>product catalogs</strong> that let you test new models without deploying code. </p></div><div id="25-saas-pricing-page-examples-and-what-makes-them-work" style="scroll-margin-top: 7.5rem;"><h2>25 SaaS pricing page examples and what makes them work</h2></div><div id="1-figma" style="scroll-margin-top: 7.5rem;"><h2>1. Figma</h2><figure style="max-width:1600pxpx" class="w-richtext-align-fullwidth w-richtext-figure-type-image"><div><img loading="lazy" alt="" src="https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6a3ac9ec801ff3df9f0d45c5/6a3acd3e295752076eea6f78_69aefc9cde1c10105035a7e8_2f583996.png"></div></figure><p>Let's start with a tool most product teams already know.</p><p><a href="https://www.figma.com/pricing/" data-wf-native-id-path="5510a16b-dd56-7958-1172-1dab1b373268" data-wf-ao-click-engagement-tracking="true" data-wf-element-id="5510a16b-dd56-7958-1172-1dab1b373268">Figma's pricing page</a> has to work for solo designers and 500-person product teams. Figma’s solution is seat-based pricing that charges differently based on role. <strong>Collab seats, Dev seats, and Full seats</strong> each have their own price within every tier.</p><p>If your developer just needs to inspect designs, they're not paying the same rate as someone building in the file. That kind of flexibility requires pricing infrastructure that can handle multiple seat types across multiple tiers without breaking.</p><p>The “Choose this plan if you …” sections below each tier also help. Instead of decoding feature lists, visitors see directly who each plan is for.</p><p><strong>What makes it work:</strong></p><ul role="list"><li>Role-based seat pricing that matches how teams actually operate</li><li>Clear indicators showing which Figma products each seat unlocks</li><li>Guidance copy that helps visitors self-select the right plan</li></ul></div><div id="2-airtable" style="scroll-margin-top: 7.5rem;"><h2>2. Airtable</h2><figure style="max-width:1600pxpx" class="w-richtext-align-fullwidth w-richtext-figure-type-image"><div><img loading="lazy" alt="" src="https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6a3ac9ec801ff3df9f0d45c5/6a3acd3e295752076eea6f66_69aefc9cde1c10105035a7eb_6557d9a5.png"></div></figure><p>Individuals tracking personal projects. Basecamp</h2><figure style="max-width:1600pxpx" class="w-richtext-align-fullwidth w-richtext-figure-type-image"><div><img loading="lazy" alt="" src="https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6a3ac9ec801ff3df9f0d45c5/6a3acd41295752076eea7088_69aefc9cde1c10105035a83b_8098a137.png"></div></figure><p><a href="https://basecamp.com/pricing" data-wf-native-id-path="49dfdee7-4b92-b126-f114-9ae31327e3d1" data-wf-ao-click-engagement-tracking="true" data-wf-element-id="49dfdee7-4b92-b126-f114-9ae31327e3d1">Basecamp's pricing page</a> looks like a landing page that happens to have prices on it.</p><p><strong>Instead of leading with tiers and feature grids, Basecamp leads with narrative:</strong> what's included in every package, why Basecamp is a company worth trusting, and how customers feel after switching. Typeform</h2><figure style="max-width:1600pxpx" class="w-richtext-align-fullwidth w-richtext-figure-type-image"><div><img loading="lazy" alt="" src="https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6a3ac9ec801ff3df9f0d45c5/6a3acd41295752076eea708e_69aefc9cde1c10105035a83e_3b785a00.png"></div></figure><p><strong>Dense pricing pages have a problem:</strong> buyers get lost before they get to the CTA. <a href="https://www.typeform.com/pricing" data-wf-native-id-path="e848c6ff-e535-2ea4-f198-6e1d2a30ff6b" data-wf-ao-click-engagement-tracking="true" data-wf-element-id="e848c6ff-e535-2ea4-f198-6e1d2a30ff6b">Typeform's page</a> is long, four tiers with comparison tables covering responses, features, integrations, and support, but Typeform has built the navigation to match.</p><p><strong>Sticky headers</strong> keep tier names visible as you scroll. A <strong>sticky footer</strong> appears at the bottom with CTAs for each plan. You can explore every detail without losing your place or hunting for the next step.</p><p><strong>Mid-scroll, a testimonial banner shows up,</strong> “Trusted by 95% of the Fortune 500.” Most pages save social proof for the top or bottom. Typeform puts it right where attention starts to drift.</p><p><strong>What makes it work:</strong></p><ul role="list"><li>Sticky headers keeping tier context visible while scrolling</li><li>Sticky footer CTAs appearing when buyers are ready to act</li><li>Social proof positioned mid-page to reinforce credibility</li></ul></div><div id="best-practices-for-saas-pricing-pages" style="scroll-margin-top: 7.5rem;"><h2>Best practices for SaaS pricing pages</h2><div class="w-embed"><div class="table-wrapper">  <table>    <thead>    <tr>      <th style="width: 33.333333333333336%;">Pricing page principle</th>      <th style="width: 33.333333333333336%;">Core goal</th>      <th style="width: 33.333333333333336%;">Why it matters</th>    </tr>    </thead>    <tbody>    <tr>      <td>Clear comparison structure</td>      <td>Reduce cognitive overload</td>      <td>Buyers can find answers quickly instead of abandoning the page</td>    </tr>    <tr>      <td>Obvious plan positioning</td>      <td>Enable self-selection</td>      <td>Buyers instantly know which plan fits them without reading every detail</td>    </tr>    <tr>      <td>Cost estimation tools</td>      <td>Improve pricing clarity</td>      <td>Usage-based buyers understand real costs before purchasing</td>    </tr>    <tr>      <td>Visible usage limits</td>      <td>Prevent surprises</td>      <td>Sets expectations and reduces support tickets</td>    </tr>    <tr>      <td>Organized FAQs</td>      <td>Speed up decision-making</td>      <td>Buyers get answers without friction or endless scrolling</td>    </tr>    <tr>      <td>Intentional trust signals</td>      <td>Build confidence</td>      <td>Reinforces credibility at key decision moments</td>    </tr>    </tbody>  </table></div></div><p><strong>Now, let’s look at what the best pricing pages get right in more detail:</strong></p><div id="they-help-buyers-compare-without-getting-lost"><h3>They help buyers compare without getting lost</h3><p><strong>Page organization</strong> determines whether users find answers in 10 seconds or give up. <strong>Sticky headers</strong> work best for long feature lists (20+ items), and tabs make sense when you have 2–4 distinct sections or plans to toggle between. <strong>Column layouts, collapsible sections, tabs.</strong> Different approaches, same goal. The best pages pick one approach and commit to it.</p></div><div id="they-make-it-obvious-who-each-plan-is-for"><h3>They make it obvious who each plan is for</h3><p>Buyers should be able to self-select without reading every line item. <strong>Taglines, feature groupings, and personas</strong> like “For growing teams” or “Built for enterprise security” let buyers skip the fine print. If someone has to study the page to figure out which plan fits them, you've already lost momentum.</p></div><div id="they-let-buyers-estimate-their-actual-cost"><h3>They let buyers estimate their actual cost</h3><p>This matters most for usage-based and credit models. If the final price depends on how much someone uses, a static table isn't going to answer their questions. <strong>Give customers an interactive calculator or slider that updates in real time</strong> so they can see what they'll actually pay across different tiers.</p></div><div id="they-show-usage-limits-up-front"><h3>They show usage limits up front</h3><p>For AI and API products, <strong>displaying limits and credit balances</strong> up front prevents billing surprises. It also saves your support team from answering the same questions over and over.</p></div><div id="they-organize-their-faqs"><h3>They organize their FAQs</h3><p>Organizing FAQs by category helps users find answers in seconds instead of scrolling through dozens of unrelated questions. <strong>Group questions into clear categories</strong> like “Billing,” “Technical Support,” “Account Management,” and “Getting Started.” Buyers scan category headings, click the relevant section, and get their answer without reading every question on the page.</p></div><div id="they-place-trust-signals-intentionally"><h3>They place trust signals intentionally</h3><p>Strategic placement of trust signals converts more visitors than disorganized social proof scattered across the page. <strong>Position customer logos and testimonials above the fold</strong> (before pricing tables) to build credibility when buyers first arrive. <strong>Add specific ROI claims or case study snippets mid-page,</strong> right after pricing details, to reinforce trust when decision fatigue sets in.</p></div></div><div id="what-happens-when-your-pricing-needs-to-change" style="scroll-margin-top: 7.5rem;"><h2>What happens when your pricing needs to change?</h2><p>Every SaaS pricing page example in this list will need an update at some point. A new tier, different limits, or an add-on that didn't exist last quarter. The question is whether that change takes an afternoon or becomes a project.</p><p>The companies that iterate fastest on pricing keep their pricing page connected to their product catalog. Update the source, and the page reflects it.</p><p>Stigg's<a href="https://docs.stigg.io/documentation/snap-in-widgets/pricing-table" data-wf-native-id-path="c0da4730-f6b5-f625-9f4f-a47a1ad3c0c3" data-wf-ao-click-engagement-tracking="true" data-wf-element-id="c0da4730-f6b5-f625-9f4f-a47a1ad3c0c3"> pricing table widget</a> works this way. Change a plan in your catalog, and your pricing page updates automatically.</p><p>If you're rethinking how your pricing page stays in sync with your stack, <a href="https://www.stigg.io/book-a-demo" data-wf-native-id-path="cda82114-34de-2a53-764d-6d372365d773" data-wf-ao-click-engagement-tracking="true" data-wf-element-id="cda82114-34de-2a53-764d-6d372365d773">we're happy to walk you through it</a>.</p></div><div id="faqs" style="scroll-margin-top: 7.5rem;"><h2>FAQs</h2><div id="1-whats-the-most-effective-layout-for-a-saas-pricing-page"><h3>1. What’s the most effective layout for a SaaS pricing page?</h3><p>The most effective layout for a SaaS pricing page depends on your catalog complexity. Side-by-side tiers work best for three to four plans. Left-hand navigation suits large product portfolios like Datadog or HubSpot, while collapsible sections help when you have multiple pricing models like Webflow.</p></div><div id="2-how-many-pricing-tiers-should-i-show"><h3>2. How many pricing tiers should I show?</h3><p>Most SaaS pricing pages show three to four pricing tiers. This range balances upsell opportunities without overwhelming buyers. The sweet spot combines Free, Pro, and Enterprise, with a highlighted “Most Popular” option to guide undecided visitors. Fewer than three limits growth, but more than four creates hesitation.</p></div><div id="3-should-i-include-a-pricing-calculator-or-slider"><h3>3. Should I include a pricing calculator or slider?</h3><p>Yes, you should include a pricing calculator or slider if your pricing depends on usage, seats, or configuration. Static tables can't answer “What will I actually pay?” for usage-based models. Sliders and calculators like those from ChartMogul, Scribe, and Zapier let buyers estimate costs based on their specific situation.</p></div><div id="4-where-should-i-place-social-proof-on-a-pricing-page"><h3>4. Where should I place social proof on a pricing page?</h3><p>Place social proof early to build confidence before buyers dig into details, and mid-page to reinforce trust when attention drifts. Logos from recognizable companies work well near the top, while customer quotes with specific metrics perform better near CTAs.</p></div><div id="5-how-do-i-update-my-pricing-page-when-plans-change"><h3>5. How do I update my pricing page when plans change?</h3><p>Updating your pricing page when plans change requires a system that automates the process. <strong>The fastest-iterating companies manage their product catalog in a system where everything updates automatically:</strong> in-app customer portals, billing, CPQ, and pricing pages. </p><p>With tools like Stigg, you update a plan once, and the changes reflect across your stack. No code changes required for new tiers, usage limits, or add-ons.</p></div></div></div><div data-social-share="" fs-copyclip-instance="2" class="blog-template_share-links"><a
